Party_Details.Name: Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd (in liquidation)

Facts.Text: Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd was at all material times a construction company undertaking large-scale public and private residential and commercial projects and was the only significant revenue-generating asset of its parent company United Fiber System Limited.

Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd was at all material times a wholly-owned subsidiary of United Fiber System Limited, a company listed on the Singapore Exchange that was primarily an investment holding company.
United Fiber System Limited approved a tender price of S$120 million for Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd to bid for the Sophia Residence project, based on an indicated margin of 6 percent.

The board of Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd approved a settlement arrangement with CCM Industrial Pte Ltd in relation to the Bishopsgate Residences project, including a final net payment of S$2 million to CCM.
Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd submitted a tender of S$115.84 million for the Sophia Residence project with a proposed contract period of 30 months from 2010-03-01 to 2012-08-31.
The Sophia Residence project contract for S$115.84 million commenced for Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd with a contract period scheduled from 2010-03-01 to 2012-08-31.
Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd was awarded the Bishopsgate Residences project by Prime Residential Development Pte Ltd at a contract sum of approximately S$58.5 million.

Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd issued a letter of award to CCM Industrial Pte Ltd for the Bishopsgate Residences project, confirming CCM as subcontractor to design, carry out, complete and maintain the works for a subcontract sum of S$51,343,900.

Kajima Overseas Asia Ltd, through its representative Gen Yasuda, sent a letter to the chief operating officer of Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd reiterating that the Bishopsgate Residences contract prohibited subcontracting the whole of the works and requesting that CCM Industrial Pte Ltd be removed from the project.

Minutes of a Bishopsgate Residences site meeting in July 2010 recorded that Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd would progressively phase out CCM Industrial Pte Ltd from the project, with an indication that this would be done by the end of July 2010.

In December 2009, Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd tendered for the Goodwood Residences condominium project at a price of S$191.93 million and the developer accepted the tender in the same month with a contract period of 30 months.

By December 2011, Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd had made progress claims amounting to about 30 percent of the total S$191.93 million contract sum for the Goodwood Residences project, while a substantial portion of the contract works remained incomplete.
In March 2012, a senior project manager of Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd was appointed as project manager of the Goodwood Residences project with responsibility for overseeing daily operations.

With approval from senior management of Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd, the Goodwood Residences project manager engaged reinforced concrete subcontractors to supply casual labourers and supervisors at hourly rates of S$12 per labourer and S$15 per supervisor.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and project management decisions, the company incurred project and financial outcomes that later formed the basis of claims against former directors and senior managers.
Party_Details.Rule: Company directors and senior managers are generally manage tenders, subcontracting, project staffing, and financial reporting with reasonable care and in accordance with contractual and corporate authorisations, so that the company’s projects and finances are not adversely affected by their decisions.
Party_Details.Application: Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd submitted tenders for three major projects at specific prices and contract periods, entered into a substantial subcontract for the Bishopsgate Residences project, engaged hourly paid labour at the Goodwood Residences project under time pressure and labour constraints, and experienced significant delays and cost overruns that preceded judicial management and liquidation. These pre-dispute events formed the factual basis for later disputes about whether the company’s leadership complied with expected standards in tendering, subcontracting, labour engagement, and financial communication.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment analyses whether former directors and senior managers of the plaintiff breached duties in relation to the Sophia Residence, Bishopsgate Residences, and Goodwood Residences projects, including issues of underpricing, total subcontracting contrary to contract terms, labour cost and supervision, back charges, extension-of-time claims, and alleged concealment of financial information from boards. It evaluates expert evidence on construction costs and labour rates, interprets contractual clauses such as clause 2.3.1 of the Bishopsgate contract, and considers causation and loss, including liquidated damages and project losses. The court distinguishes between pleaded and unpleaded allegations, assesses credibility of witnesses, and determines which individuals are responsible for specific contractual and management decisions. It also addresses a third-party contribution claim against former founders and investor representatives, and ultimately reaches findings on liability, causation, quantum, and contribution that are not to be used as pre-dispute facts.

Party_Details.Name: Lauw Wisanggeni

Facts.Text: In February or March 2009, Lauw Wisanggeni was appointed executive chairman and executive director of Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd with a starting salary of S$50,000 per month.

Before his appointment to Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd, Lauw Wisanggeni provided consultancy services to United Fiber System Limited on forestry developments, which were unrelated to the construction business of Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd.
On 2009-11-10, a tender for the Sophia Residence project at S$115.84 million submitted by Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd was signed by Lauw Wisanggeni together with the chief operating officer.
In early November 2009, Lauw Wisanggeni sought approval from the board of United Fiber System Limited to tender for the Sophia Residence project at S$120 million, indicating a margin of 6 percent.

In December 2009, the tender for the Goodwood Residences project at S$191.93 million submitted by Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd was signed by Lauw Wisanggeni and the chief operating officer and was accepted by the developer in the same month.
In April 2010, Lauw Wisanggeni entered into an option to purchase a residential unit in Goodwood Residences for a purchase price of S$8.68 million.
In July 2013, Lauw Wisanggeni sold his Goodwood Residences unit for S$10.6 million.

In August 2011, the chief operating officer emailed Lauw Wisanggeni describing the Bishopsgate Residences project award, the arrangement to subcontract a majority of the project to CCM Industrial Pte Ltd on a back-to-back basis, and a proposed settlement with CCM for a final net payment of S$2 million, and sought board approval for this settlement.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the executive chairman’s decisions and approvals in relation to tender pricing, subcontracting arrangements, labour engagement approvals, and acquisition of a unit in a client’s development were consistent with expected standards for senior management of a construction company.
Party_Details.Rule: Senior executives of a company are generally ensure that tenders and subcontracting arrangements are authorised, that project decisions are made with reasonable care in light of available cost information and contractual terms, and that personal transactions connected to company projects are handled transparently to avoid conflicts with corporate governance expectations.
Party_Details.Application: Lauw Wisanggeni approved and signed tenders for the Sophia Residence and Goodwood Residences projects at specified prices, sought and obtained board-level approval for a Sophia Residence tender price, approved a settlement with a major subcontractor on the Bishopsgate Residences project after a back-to-back subcontracting arrangement, and personally purchased and later sold a unit in the Goodwood Residences development. These actions occurred while he held the roles of executive chairman and executive director and interacted with both the company’s and the parent company’s boards, forming the factual basis for later evaluation of his adherence to expected executive standards.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court evaluates whether Lauw Wisanggeni breached director duties in relation to the Sophia Residence tender pricing, the Bishopsgate Residences subcontract to CCM Industrial Pte Ltd, the Goodwood Residences labour and cost issues, alleged concealment of project losses and cash flow information from boards, and the acquisition and sale of a Goodwood Residences unit. It considers his claimed role as a rain-maker without construction expertise, his reliance on other managers, and whether he authorised or knew of a total subcontract in breach of contract. The judgment also addresses whether his personal property transaction created a conflict or undisclosed profit and whether any such findings result in liability.

Party_Details.Name: Leong Chee Keng

Facts.Text: On 2009-06-01, Leong Chee Keng was appointed chief operating officer of Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd with a starting salary of S$21,000 per month.
In December 2009, Leong Chee Keng was appointed a director of Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd.
On 2009-11-10, the Sophia Residence project tender at S$115.84 million submitted by Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd was signed by Leong Chee Keng together with the executive chairman.

In December 2009, the Goodwood Residences project tender at S$191.93 million submitted by Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd was signed by Leong Chee Keng and the executive chairman and accepted by the developer in the same month.

On 2010-03-11, a representative of CCM Industrial Pte Ltd emailed a tender of S$50,998,000 to Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd via the senior project manager for the design and construction of the Bishopsgate Residences project.

On 2010-05-20, Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd issued a letter of award to CCM Industrial Pte Ltd confirming acceptance of CCM as subcontractor for the Bishopsgate Residences project for S$51,343,900.

On 2010-07-02, Gen Yasuda of Kajima Overseas Asia Ltd sent a letter to Leong Chee Keng stating that the Bishopsgate Residences contract prohibited subcontracting the whole of the works and requesting that CCM Industrial Pte Ltd be removed from the project to avoid the impression that CCM was the main contractor.

Minutes of a Bishopsgate Residences site meeting in July 2010 recorded that Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd would progressively phase out CCM Industrial Pte Ltd from the project by the end of July 2010, with agreement recorded from the contracts manager.

In August 2011, Leong Chee Keng emailed Lauw Wisanggeni describing that Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd had been awarded the Bishopsgate Residences project for S$58.5 million, that CCM Industrial Pte Ltd had been invited to work on the tender on a back-to-back subcontract basis, and proposing a final settlement with CCM for S$2 million including an ex gratia component.

In August 2011, the management of Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd, including Leong Chee Keng, sought and obtained board approval to enter into a settlement arrangement with CCM Industrial Pte Ltd for the Bishopsgate Residences project involving a final net payment of S$2 million.
In August 2010, Leong Chee Keng gave instructions to CCM Industrial Pte Ltd regarding engagement of subcontractors for mechanical and engineering works on the Bishopsgate Residences project.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the chief operating officer’s involvement in tender submissions, subcontract awards, communications with the developer, and settlement arrangements for the Bishopsgate Residences and other projects aligned with expected standards for operational management in a construction company.
Party_Details.Rule: Operational directors are generally ensure that project tenders and subcontracting comply with main contract terms, that communications with developers address concerns about subcontracting arrangements, and that settlements with subcontractors are made with reasonable regard to project obligations and company interests.
Party_Details.Application: Leong Chee Keng signed tenders for the Sophia Residence and Goodwood Residences projects, participated in awarding a substantial subcontract to CCM Industrial Pte Ltd for the Bishopsgate Residences project, received a letter from the developer’s representative reiterating a prohibition on subcontracting the whole of the works, and later proposed and obtained approval for a S$2 million settlement with CCM. These pre-dispute actions form the factual basis for assessing whether his operational decisions were consistent with expected standards under the project contracts and corporate governance.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment considers whether Leong Chee Keng was responsible for causing a total subcontract of the Bishopsgate Residences project to CCM Industrial Pte Ltd in breach of a contractual prohibition, and whether his subsequent handling of the developer’s objections and the settlement with CCM met his duties. It also examines his role in the Sophia Residence and Goodwood Residences projects, including labour and cost issues, and whether any findings of breach lead to liability and loss. The court’s conclusions on these matters are not to be treated as pre-dispute facts.

Party_Details.Name: Ng Giok Beng

Facts.Text: On 2009-10-01, Ng Giok Beng commenced employment with Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd as a senior project manager with a starting salary of S$9,500 per month.
On 2010-03-11, Ng Giok Beng received by email from a representative of CCM Industrial Pte Ltd a tender of S$50,998,000 for the design and construction of the Bishopsgate Residences project.
In March 2012, Ng Giok Beng was appointed project manager of the Goodwood Residences project with responsibility for overseeing daily operations.

With approval from senior management, Ng Giok Beng engaged reinforced concrete subcontractors to provide casual labourers and supervisors for the Goodwood Residences project at hourly rates of S$12 per labourer and S$15 per supervisor.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the senior project manager’s handling of subcontractor tenders and labour engagement on major projects was consistent with expected project management standards in the construction industry.
Party_Details.Rule: Project managers are generally manage subcontractor inputs and site labour in a manner consistent with project requirements, cost considerations, and instructions from senior management, while implementing reasonable site-level controls and deployment practices.
Party_Details.Application: Ng Giok Beng received CCM Industrial Pte Ltd’s tender for the Bishopsgate Residences project and later, as project manager for the Goodwood Residences project, arranged for hourly paid labour and supervisors under senior management approval. These pre-dispute actions form the factual basis for later assessment of whether his project management conduct aligned with expected standards for cost and site control.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment examines whether Ng Giok Beng was implicated in any total subcontracting arrangement for the Bishopsgate Residences project and whether his management of hourly paid labour at the Goodwood Residences project met required standards. It notes that certain claims against him were not pursued to conclusion and assesses his role primarily in the context of site operations rather than strategic decisions.

Party_Details.Name: Chia Quee Hock

Facts.Text: In 1975, Chia Quee Hock founded Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd.
Around 1983, Chia Quee Hock became managing director of Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd.
In 2001, Chia Quee Hock was appointed deputy chairman of United Fiber System Limited.
In 2009, Chia Quee Hock became a non-executive director of United Fiber System Limited.
Chia Quee Hock does not read, speak or understand English and relied on others for English-language documents.
The Conditions of Contract for the Sophia Residence project were signed by Chia Quee Hock together with the chief operating officer of Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the founder and former managing director’s later roles in the group and involvement in contract execution for the Sophia Residence project had any bearing on responsibility for project and financial outcomes.
Party_Details.Rule: Non-executive and group-level directors who sign project contracts are generally understand or obtain explanations of key commercial terms and to rely on management for operational execution, within the framework of corporate governance.
Party_Details.Application: Chia Quee Hock founded the company, later held senior roles in the parent company, and signed the Conditions of Contract for the Sophia Residence project while not being fluent in English. These pre-dispute facts were later considered in assessing the extent to which he shared responsibility for project decisions primarily driven by executive management.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment considers whether Chia Quee Hock, as founder and signatory to certain contracts, should contribute to any liability arising from project underpricing or subcontracting decisions. It evaluates his knowledge of tender prices and subcontracting arrangements and whether his non-executive and language-limited role mitigated responsibility.

Party_Details.Name: Peh Pit Tat

Facts.Text: On 2011-06-01, Peh Pit Tat, vice-president of Argyle Street Management Limited, joined the board of United Fiber System Limited as a non-executive director.
In July 2011, Peh Pit Tat was appointed to the board of Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd as a director without drawing a salary.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the non-executive director’s actions in relation to board participation and initiating judicial management proceedings affected the company’s eventual financial outcome.
Party_Details.Rule: Non-executive directors involved in restructuring decisions are generally act on available financial information and to consider options such as judicial management in light of the company’s solvency and creditor interests.
Party_Details.Application: After joining the boards of United Fiber System Limited and Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd, Peh Pit Tat later initiated judicial management proceedings for Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd based on concerns about cash flow and debt repayment. These pre-dispute and pre-liquidation actions formed part of the factual background for later assessment of the impact of restructuring decisions on the company’s outcome.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment addresses whether Peh Pit Tat should contribute to any liability on the basis that he did not secure repayment of intercompany loans, participated in project-related decisions, or applied for judicial management in a way that allegedly undermined the company’s prospects. It considers the legal effect of the judicial management order and whether criticisms of the restructuring process are barred or unfounded.

Party_Details.Name: Chan Kin

Facts.Text: On 2011-05-01, Chan Kin, chief investment officer of Argyle Street Management Limited, joined the board of United Fiber System Limited as a non-executive director.
In July 2011, Chan Kin was appointed to the board of Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd as a director without drawing a salary.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the non-executive director’s board roles in the parent and subsidiary companies had any bearing on responsibility for project and financial decisions that preceded the company’s insolvency.
Party_Details.Rule: Non-executive directors are generally oversee management and participate in key decisions but are not typically involved in day-to-day project operations, with their responsibility assessed in light of their oversight role and information provided by management.
Party_Details.Application: After joining the boards of United Fiber System Limited and Poh Lian Construction (Pte.) Ltd, Chan Kin participated in board-level oversight while executive management handled tenders, subcontracting, and project operations. These pre-dispute facts were later considered in evaluating whether he shared responsibility for outcomes primarily driven by operational decisions.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment considers whether Chan Kin, as a non-executive director associated with an investor, should contribute to any liability arising from project and financial management, including decisions about labour arrangements and restructuring. It evaluates the extent of his involvement in operational matters and whether his oversight role gave rise to any legal responsibility for the losses claimed.
